data.title: Middle East War Pressures Copper Prices, Could Impact Nickel Production
data.description:
Due to the broadening conflict in the Middle East, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz on Feb. 28 led to increased maritime risks for oil and energy markets. With over 25% of global seaborne oil and about 20% of global LNG trade passing through the strait, this stalled trade route furthers cost-push inflation given supply and logistical uncertainties.

In this article, we highlight the closure's impacts to the copper and nickel markets, with the latter becoming increasingly susceptible to supply-side shocks.

Copper prices tempered by US dollar

The escalation of the US-Iran conflict is expected to have a limited effect on global copper supply. Although Iran is the Middle East's main copper producer, its output - about 360,000 metric tons of mined copper and 340,000 mt of refined copper in 2025 - represents just 1.5% and 1.2% of global production, respectively.

In 2025, Iran exported just over 109,000 mt of refined copper, mainly to the United Arab Emirates and Turkey. Both countries can easily source copper from alternative suppliers, minimizing the risk of disruption.

Global refined copper stocks have increased significantly in major exchange warehouses since January, rising 50% by early March to 1.25 million mt. This inventory buildup provides a buffer against potential supply shocks from the region.

The London Metal Exchange three-month (LME 3M) copper price fell from $13,343.5/mt on Feb. 27 to $12,955/mt on March 3, suggesting the conflict's immediate impact on copper has been downward. The closure of the strait has triggered a rapid increase in energy prices, which is expected to weaken global growth and put pressure on copper demand.

At the same time, higher energy prices have renewed concerns about inflation, potentially delaying any US Federal Reserve interest rate cuts. This has strengthened the US dollar recently, adding further downward pressure on copper prices.

High global refined copper stocks are also weighing on prices, but this could be a short-term phenomenon. With the Chinese market now back from the Lunar New Year holiday, copper-consuming sectors are resuming production and gradually entering their seasonal peak demand period.

As a result, we anticipate stocks at the Shanghai Futures Exchange will begin to decline from current high levels. Most global copper stocks are now sitting in the US market and are unlikely to flow out easily, especially with the possibility of the US imposing tariffs on refined copper imports.

In summary, copper prices are currently under short-term pressure from a stronger dollar, high inventories and demand concerns should global GDP growth weaken. Looking ahead, potential dollar weakness and ongoing tightness in the concentrates market are expected to provide support for copper prices.

Nickel supply chain vulnerable given Hormuz closure

The closure of the Strait of Hormuz highlighted a critical vulnerability within the global nickel value chain: the availability and cost stability of sulfur-based inputs essential for high-pressure acid leach (HPAL) operations. HPAL technology is a vital processing stage in the production of battery-grade nickel sulfate (NiSO₄) used in electric vehicle batteries.

It relies heavily on sulfuric acid to convert laterite ore into mixed hydroxide precipitate (MHP), which is subsequently turned into NiSO₄. Thus, disruptions in the sulfur supply chain can materially impact primary nickel supply and even exacerbate price volatility.

According to S&P Global Energy's preliminary assessment, Indonesia imported over 5.2 million mt of sulfur in 2025, up by 44% year over year, driven by the accelerated expansion of HPAL output. Exports to Indonesia from Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the UAE jumped 29%, led by outflows toward Bahodopi and Weda Bay, both of which house HPAL plants.

Bahodopi, a joint venture between PT Vale Indonesia Tbk (PTNDF) and GEM Hong Kong International Co. Ltd., is expected to commence operations within the year. On the other hand, PT Weda Bay Nickel received a lower production quota of 12 million wet metric tons for 2026, from 42 million wmt in 2025. Eramet SA (ERMAF), one of the venturers alongside Tsingshan Holding Group Co. Ltd. and PT Aneka Tambang Tbk (PAEKY), stated that the company will apply for a quota increase.

Although the recent rally in LME 3M nickel prices within the $17,000/mt to $18,000/mt range - partly attributed to Indonesia's recent quota reduction announcements - has improved market sentiment, it does not fully offset the impact of rising sulfur costs.

HPAL plants with higher all‑in sustaining costs (AISC) in regions such as Australia and New Caledonia are more vulnerable during a surge in sulfur prices. These plants often struggle with design limitations, slow ramp‑up performance and persistently high operating costs.

By contrast, Indonesia's HPAL operations benefit from structural advantages that keep their AISC low. They operate with lower energy and labor costs and tend to achieve higher recoveries through newer-generation HPAL designs.

Should the disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz persist, the nickel market will likely begin to increase the price premium associated with battery-grade nickel chemicals. The next phase of nickel market volatility may be driven less by ore supply constraints and more by the resilience of the sulfur-to-acid supply chain that underpins HPAL operations.

Original Post

data.title: Blue Owl Capital Corporation Isn't Worth A Hoot
data.description:
High yield opportunities can be difficult to resist. Even though I myself don't care much about payouts to investors, even I acknowledge that if the yield is high enough, it can be difficult to say no to. Unfortunately, there are many opportunities that exist out there in the market that offer large payouts but that are, on the whole, subpar investments. One great example of this has historically been Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). Today, the company offers a yield of 13.6%. Considering that the S&P 500 usually averages between 10% and 11% annually, that looks promising at face value.

Even so, back in December of last year, I ended up rating this enterprise a ‘sell.’ I was discouraged by a couple of things. For starters, the vast majority of the debt that the company owned, which comprised about 83% of its portfolio at the time, was floating rate. That meant that, as interest rates dropped, it should see a decline in profitability. But on top of this, the enterprise already had a track record of underperforming the market. And this wasn't just over a short window of time. It was actually over the course of many years. With interest rates slated to drop, I argued that further downward pressure could be put onto its profitability. And while we have not actually seen that come to pass, my overall stance on the matter remains unchanged. Add on top of this a little bit of drama that recently broke out regarding its family of businesses, and I think that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ candidate is the right move here.

Digging into Blue Owl Capital

At its core, Blue Owl Capital is a conceptually interesting company. The company exists for the primary purpose of originating and making loans to middle-market companies in the US. These are primarily smaller, privately held businesses, that are trying to access capital for cheaper than what other alternatives might be. At the end of 2025, the company had investments in 234 different businesses. These were spread across 30 different industries, with the largest being the Internet software and services space at 11.1% of total exposure. Other major categories include health care providers and services, asset based lending and fund financing firms, insurance businesses, health care technology, manufacturing, and food and beverage enterprises. It is worth noting that the Internet software and services portion of the company's emphasis could be very appealing if forecasts regarding AI turn out to be accurate. In 2023, global cloud spending amounted to $496 billion. By 2030 it is expected to explode to $2 trillion. However, as I detailed in another article recently, there does appear to be an AI bubble that, if I turn out to be correct, could lead to increased risk for the business.

The middle market space is actually quite large. Even if we just focus on the US, there are an estimated 200,000 such companies that employ around 48 million people. In fact, these businesses collectively account for around one-third of private sector GDP. Management specifically targets those businesses that generate EBITDA of between $25 million and $500 million, as well as those that generate revenue of between $125 million and $5 billion. This is primarily what you would consider to be the upper middle market space. This excludes firms that, by definition, can be as small as $10 million per year worth of EBITDA. But by focusing on these more sizable enterprises, the overall risk profile should be reduced compared to if the firm were to invest in enterprises at the low end of the range.

Management has made sure, over time, heavily diversify the company. Its top ten largest positions account for only 23% of its portfolio. What’s more, 73% of its portfolio is invested in first lien debt, while 5% is tied up in second lien debt. All told, about 79% of its portfolio is invested in senior secured securities. Outside of that, it does have some exposure to preferred equity, unsecured debt, common equity, and joint ventures. But when you add all of this together, we end up with a well-diversified prospect that, thanks to the business model of the company, allows it to capture a pretty hefty yield.

As interest rates have dropped, we have started to see a decline in the weighted average total yield that the company has been able to get from its investments. Back in 2023, for instance, this was 11.9% per annum. But by the end of 2025, that number had fallen to 9.5%. This might seem small. But when we are talking about a portfolio that consists of $16.47 billion worth of assets, it adds up in a big way. Management does help to boost returns by taking on a bit of leverage. But the net debt-to-equity ratio of the company at the end of 2025 was about 1.19. That's not exactly high. But it does help to explain the greater yield that the company is currently paying out.

One thing that has been helpful for shareholders is the fact that, over time, management has succeeded in growing overall net assets. The firm ended 2025 with $7.40 billion worth, with investments at fair value worth $16.47 billion. These numbers compare favorably to the $5.95 billion and $13.19 billion, respectively, that the firm had at the end of 2024. This has allowed a general increase in financial performance. In fact, in the chart below, you can see investment income, net investment income, unrealized gains, realized gains, and a change in net assets, for the company for the last four completed fiscal years now. 2025 saw a $627.4 million increase in net assets for the company thanks to robust net investment income and a very small uptick in unrealized gains. The picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that unrealized losses totaled $179.2 million.

To better understand what is going on here, I would like to point you to the chart below. In it, you can see dividends, changes in net assets, distributions, and other metrics, that I calculated for the last few years now. What we see here is that the company continues to pay out its change in net assets to shareholders in the form of distributions. That's not surprising. In fact, that's the entire point of this business model. It's also the only mechanism through which it's yield could be even remotely close to as high as it is now.

The problem with this approach, however, is that in order to grow, Blue Owl Capital must necessarily issue additional shares. In fact, from the final quarter of 2024 to the final quarter of 2025, the overall number of shares outstanding jumped 28%. In many instances that I have looked at in the past, this means a deterioration of shareholder value. But as the chart below illustrates, NAV per share has been pretty consistent. Admittedly, the $15.26 reported for the end of 2024 was higher than the $14.81 that the company had in 2025. And when you consider that, lately, the price per unit for the company has been a bit on the weak side of things, the end result has been a bit of deterioration in shareholder value. But this is not massive in the grand scheme of things, especially compared to other investments similar to this that I have analyzed in the past. In those cases, I often assign the companies or funds in question a ‘strong sell’ rating.

Some of the pressure that the company has likely faced lately has come from some drama regarding Blue Owl Capital Corporation II, which is a privately held BDC that is also managed by the parent business known as Blue Owl Capital. This is a fairly illiquid retail private credit fund. Earlier this year, the parent, Blue Owl Capital, shut the door on investor withdrawals from that fund and ended quarterly redemptions. This was rather significant news, with Mohamed El-Erian, who is one of the most well-known individuals in finance, warning that this could be a ‘canary in the coal mine’ moment for the private credit market. The private credit space is estimated to be worth around $3 trillion, making it a major segment of global finance. And by the year 2030, it's expected to expand further to $4 trillion in size. But since the final quarter of 2025, withdrawal requests for large BDCs have surged 200%. This comes as ‘shadow default rates’ for companies that use payment and kind debt have more than doubled since 2021. Some of this is likely because of broader economic weakness. However, declining interest rates are compressing overall returns, as I detailed already.

After this decision by management, Blue Owl Capital made the decision to sell $1.4 billion of its loans in order to raise liquidity. At first glance, this seemed great. This was especially because these assets were sold at 99.7% of par value. Some of the drama, however, comes from the fact that $400 million of this $1.4 billion sale came from Blue Owl Capital specifically. Plus, one of the buyers was none other than an affiliate of Blue Owl Capital. This likely stoked fears that the same family of companies is shifting capital around in order to reduce concerns about the overall liquidity of the businesses in question. This can be perceived as being very similar to moving around the chairs on the deck of the Titanic. To be clear, this does not mean that Blue Owl Capital itself is at financial risk. But the optics of it were not exactly great.

Another problem is that, historically, Blue Owl Capital has been a loser. In the chart above, you can see its overall return compared to the S&P 500 and even the equal weighted index for the S&P 500 over the last few years. And in the chart below, you can see what its return has been relative to these indices since I last wrote about it in December of last year. Considering that I view a ‘sell’ candidate as one that should underperform the market for the foreseeable future, the enterprise does seem to be matching that description quite nicely.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I would argue that Blue Owl Capital still does not make sense for investors to own. It continues to perform poorly, even with the hefty yield. We also have drama and broader economic conditions that should weigh on it moving forward. This combination of factors justifies a more cautious approach in my view. That's why I think that maintaining it as a ‘sell’ candidate is the right choice.

data.title: Janus Henderson Opportunistic Alpha Managed Account Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned -3.67% (gross) and the S&P 500® Index returned 2.66%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the information technology sector detracted from relative performance. Stock selection in the consumer staples sector contributed.

Outlook

Our differentiated investment approach aims to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the broad economy.

Investment environment

  • U.S. shares rose on positive corporate earnings news. Economic growth has been relatively stable despite a government shutdown, policy headwinds, and slower-than-expected employment growth.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) followed a September rate cut with two additional 25 basis-point (bp) rate cuts, in October and December, respectively.
  • Excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) was a strong driver of returns early in the quarter. The market began to broaden in the second half of October as concerns over valuations and potential investment returns led to declines in AI-related stocks. Nonetheless, stocks ended the year on a strong note.

Portfolio review

Oracle (ORCL) was relative detractor, amid volatility in AI-related stocks. Oracle has emerged as a leading player in the AI market, supported by its strong and growing position in the hyperscale market as well as close relationships with leading AI partners. Oracle's cloud business has signed several multibillion-dollar contracts. This led to a large increase in remaining performance obligations (RPOs), which represent expected future revenues. Despite the company's strong financial performance, the stock declined in the fourth quarter as investors grew more cautious about Oracle's ability to fund the ambitious infrastructure buildout necessary to service these future contracts. Further, the market has concerns about the potential customer concentration within these RPOs. We believe these concerns are overstated as Oracle has multiple avenues for funding growth due to its solid balance sheet and strong operating cash flow. In our view, the company remains well positioned to benefit from the ongoing buildout of AI capacity because of its technological advantages and strategic business relationships.

Surgery Partners (SGRY), another relative detractor, is the largest U.S. owner and operator of ambulatory surgical centers (ASCs). The company has benefited from a growing shift in surgery volumes away from hospitals and toward ASC facilities, which offer advantages for doctors, patients, and insurers compared to hospital-based procedures. While the company reported solid third-quarter earnings, shares declined after the management team warned of near-term headwinds for its commercial business. Investors also have worried about how the suspension of Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies may impact demand for medical procedures more broadly. We believe these concerns were overstated. Surgery Partners has minimal exposure to the ACA side of the market, and we have continued to see signs of stable growth in its core market. We remain invested in the stock.

Capital One Financial (COF) was a top positive contributor. The financial services company has delivered strong performance, aided by robust credit demand and improved credit metrics. Its recent acquisition of Discover Financial Services has enhanced its competitive positioning while diversifying its business platform and expanding its technological capabilities. This merger also has allowed Capital One to move a significant amount of its debit card volumes onto the Discover network, which provides synergies and margin benefits. The company also has been returning money to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks.

Madrigal Pharmaceuticals was another relative contributor. Madrigal has become a leader in the treatment of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), which is rapidly becoming one of the leading causes of liver failure in the U.S. The company has seen strong sales of Rezdiffra, the first FDA-approved medication for MASH that targets the accumulation of liver fat and inflammation. Sentiment around the drug has been positive, driven by strong physician adoption and payer reception trends.

Manager outlook

We are optimistic about the market environment in 2026, given the backdrop of continued stable economic growth. Consumer spending has remained resilient, especially for upper-income households benefiting from asset appreciation. While job growth has slowed, the unemployment rate remains below historic averages. Slower employment growth may also give the Fed more leeway to ease monetary policy, and lower interest rates could provide an economic tailwind for a broad range of companies. We see other potential drivers for market gains, including deregulation, tax policy changes, and a more accommodative environment for mergers and acquisitions.

We have continued to look for opportunity with long-term themes such as AI, which we see persisting regardless of macroeconomic conditions. Hyperscalers are still expanding capacity, and we believe the next phase of growth could shift toward software and data-rich companies that can monetize AI through productivity gains and new services.

While we continue to monitor such themes along with macroeconomic developments, our primary focus remains bottom-up stock selection. Our differentiated investment process aims to provide broad exposure across segments, sizes, and styles, avoiding reliance on any single theme. The core of our process seeks to unlock value through differentiated insights, especially around misunderstood business models, undervalued assets, and unappreciated growth potential. We remain on the lookout for opportunities to take advantage of market volatility, as our long-term investment horizon and independent process allow us to capitalize on opportunities other investors might miss. Through this approach, we hope to provide equity diversification for clients, along with returns not tied to an index or the broad economy.

data.title: NTG Clarity Networks: A Compounding IT Platform Mispriced As A Lumpy Contractor
data.description:
Investment Summary

NTG Clarity Networks (NYWKF, NCI:CA) is a Canadian-led, Egypt-based, and Saudi-focused digital transformation provider benefiting from strong tailwinds in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

The recent weakness in margins and cash flow appears more temporary than structural. They hired staff ahead of demand to meet contract delivery, which came slightly later than expected. The key question in FY26 is whether NTG can convert its pre-built capacity into revenue, allowing margins and cash conversion to improve.

At around 0.55x sales, 3.8x EV/EBITDA, and ~6x earnings, the market prices the stock like a slow-growth, project-based contractor that is too concentrated geographically and across key accounts with limited conversion visibility. We believe this valuation fails to reflect NTG's long-term growth trajectory, margin normalization potential, contract momentum, and structurally advantaged operating model.

Business Overview

NTG Clarity Networks is a digital transformation and IT services provider. It serves telecom operators, enterprises, banks, governments, and utilities. It operates mostly in Saudi Arabia and the Gulf.

NTG has two main business lines. First, they provide digital transformation development services, including full-stack software engineering, business analysis, project management, and quality control testing. They deliver these services through a hybrid model with offshore delivery from Egypt for cost efficiency (50% customer savings over on-site services) with on-site execution in Saudi Arabia.

They also provide proprietary software products and solutions through NTGApps, their no-code platform that provides custom app templates for companies to either deploy ready-made solutions or build new apps covering ERP, IT ticketing, telecom network management, asset tracking, etc. NTGApps is not SaaS, but it helps NTG productize parts of its delivery model to improve repeatability, shorten implementation time, customer stickiness, and potentially improve economics over time. It was 6% of revenue in FY24 and more than 15% in some quarters in FY25.

NTG's growth engine goes beyond typical outbound-driven activity as it increasingly leverages its network in Saudi Arabia for commercial expansion, thanks to its 20+ year track record in the region. The business generates over 95% of its revenue from Saudi Arabia. The Kingdom's Vision 2030 aims to diversify Saudi's economy away from oil and gas. This supports demand for digital infrastructure modernization. Saudi's digital economy is already worth $132B with a CAGR of 8.5%.

NTG is an execution partner working with large Saudi system integrators and enterprise clients. It mostly finds its work within that ecosystem rather than bidding directly for mega projects. In Saudi Arabia, large national integrators (who benefit from national preference) would typically win a contract and outsource some of the work to execution specialists like NTG, which benefits from major spending flows. This forms a key part of NTG's commercial moat.

Their main economic moat is their offshore center in Egypt. It is low cost with a cost base in Egyptian pounds, while the billing is often linked to Gulf currencies, which are pegged to the USD.

Their Egyptian workforce is made of a deep talent pool thanks to NTG's university links and its own internal training programs (NTG Academy). These skilled workers share a common Arabic language, cultural proximity, and timezone. NTG also invests in local Saudi talent. This model allows NTG to have onshore and offshore execution capabilities with lower delivery costs, which offers a clear economic advantage.

More recently, NTG has also expanded beyond its initial scope of intervention (telecom and financial IT transformation) by expanding into IoT, Smart City, and hospitality/real estate digitization. This enlarges its addressable market.

What the Market Thinks

NTG is viewed as a volatile micro-cap contractor that is project-based, with sometimes long payment cycles and contract lumpiness.

Margins compressed in FY25 due to NTG hiring staff, expanding geographically, and mobilizing resources ahead of expected contract wins. This represents a short-term execution mismatch, but it is likely temporary. Operating cash flow has been affected by contract assets and receivables. A diminishing sequential revenue growth with a slightly declining backlog over the past year made the market question NTG's scalability potential and business momentum moving forward.

NTG did a LIFE offering in July 2025, which improved liquidity. That said, share count increased by roughly 12% over the year, with the majority of the dilution coming from the fundraise. It also indicated that liquidity has been tight over the past few months. More recently, IT-related stocks have taken a hit with the AI-fear-related wave. Management sees AI as a delivery and productivity enhancer, with NTG already deploying AI features within NTGApps or through Agent Builder. The current geopolitical events in Iran do also create headline risk for all businesses that have large exposure to the Middle East region.

The market may be underestimating NTG's expansion within existing accounts. Contract sizes are getting larger with longer durations. New customer acquisition increasingly comes from network referrals rather than pure outbound activity. Among others, the February 2026 frame agreement they signed came through referral, suggesting this shift is structural and not anecdotal.

What the Numbers Actually Show

NTG has scaled rapidly from CAD$17.7M in FY22 to roughly CAD$77M TTM by Q3/25. FY25 revenue guidance of CAD$78M implies strong yearly growth from FY24's CAD$56.1M. The 3-year revenue CAGR stands above 60%.

In Q3/25, revenue was CAD$20.9M (42% YoY growth). Gross margin was 34.9%, while adjusted EBITDA was CAD$2.5M. The decline in net income was impacted by taxes on the quarter, as net income before taxes was CAD$2.4M in Q3/25 vs. CAD$2.1M in Q3/24, showing that underlying profitability remained strong. Adjusted EBITDA margin was 12% in Q3/25 vs. 15% in Q2/25. This came from aggressive reinvestment in the business for future growth, namely geographic buildout costs and hiring ahead of contract conversion. As utilization of the workforce improves, margins should trend back toward historical levels, which were at a 22% average in FY24. NTGApps grew 685% YoY in Q3/25, +562% YTD, or 13% of YTD revenue, showing great traction.

The combination of roughly 90% client retention with 150% net revenue retention is one of the strongest signals in the story. Only one customer has churned so far, and the total client count is still up roughly 7% net in Q3/25. Roughly 85% of work is recurring or reoccurring, and 63% of customers increased their engagement scope YTD (against 54% in Q4/24), showing that clients not only renew their engagements but increasingly expand them over time through increasing contract size and length.

A few years back, contracts used to be in the CAD$2M to CAD$4M range; now the new announcements are usually above CAD$10M, including examples like CAD$53M or CAD$14.1M (renewals, expansions, purchase orders), or the March 2026 framework agreement ceiling of CAD$18.2M (with no minimum spend commitment). The latter is a large, strategic work, but the market is rational to wait for purchase order conversion.

The CAD$22M contract shown in the graph above was a renewal and expansion of an existing agreement. It involves a rough 25% increase in contracted resource headcount, which highlights NTG's ability to ramp up delivery capacity if need be.

NTG has been diversifying its revenue sources, with the rest-of-Middle East (Iraq, Oman, Egypt) revenue reaching CAD$633K, or roughly 3% of revenue, but growing 50% YoY. While still small, this demonstrates that NTG can replicate its model in other countries. More specifically about Iraq, about CAD$1.6M of the CAD$2.0M NTGApps-related POs signed in February 2026 were from Iraqi customers, showing traction there.

In Q3/25, operating cash flow was impacted by the timing of collections, as roughly one third of the revenue recognized was not yet billed. This was temporary, as NTG declared having collected many receivables they were owed, boosting working capital going into FY26. Management already released a notice saying they expect positive operating cash flow in Q4/25.

The key metric we focus on is the decline in backlog between year-end 2024 (roughly CAD$105M) and backlog as reported in Q3/25 (roughly CAD$88M) despite strong YoY revenue growth between FY24 and FY25. Management attributes this to a longer-than-expected sales cycle, but this does imply weaker near-term visibility. The contracts and frameworks signed post-Q3 should boost this figure, but it will be difficult for NTG to sustain its double-digit growth trajectory unless the backlog materially improves from here. Since backlog typically converts into revenue over time, reaching CAD$90M in annual revenue in FY26 (our conservative target, more below) would likely necessitate a backlog well above CAD$100M by early 2026. Backlog evolution will therefore be the key leading indicator to monitor as it drives both revenue visibility and margin normalization.

The numbers are not flawless but continue to point toward a business that is compounding, winning larger work despite temporary underutilization of the workforce. FY25 was the year of building delivery capacity; FY26 should increasingly be the year when that capacity gets used, clearly elevating the earnings base, thereby helping relieve pressure on margin and cash flow.

Valuation

After a close to 70% price decline, NTG Clarity Networks appears to be priced very cheaply. This is not Growth-At-Reasonable-Price but appears to be Growth-At-Dislocated-Price.

NTG is being perceived as a lumpy contractor, even though it has increasingly become a high-retention, embedded digital transformation platform. The current multiples suggest there will not be a meaningful improvement in utilization, margin normalization, or cash conversion while giving little value to NTG's commercial traction, larger contract sizes, referral-led replication, and the expansion into adjacent Middle Eastern markets. It seems to completely discount NTGApps and AI-related tools improving productivity or the rollout of large purchase orders entering into FY26.

Company P/S (TTM) EV/EBITDA (TTM) EBITDA Growth YoY Sales Growth YoY P/E (TTM)
NTG Clarity Networks (NYWKF, NCI:CA) 0.55x 3.8x 83.66% 62.61% 6.50x
Amdocs (DOX) 1.56x 7.57x 4.75% -5.98% 12.06x
Enghouse Systems (EGHSF) 1.81x 5.97x -6.70% -2.18% 12.91x
CSG Systems* (CSGS) 1.82x 13.56x 8.71% 2.18% 40.59x

*CSG is in the process of being acquired at an already set price. The data here is therefore not directly comparable to trading multiples.

These valuation metrics were extracted from the Seeking Alpha valuation and growth tabs for each of the stocks mentioned above.

Looking at the revenue trajectory and management's guidance for EBITDA moving forward, we believe the company could come close to the CAD$90M mark, or roughly 15% YoY growth for FY26 (conservative given their historical growth), and an 18% adjusted EBITDA margin (as utilization improves).

At today's price, it would imply a forward EV/EBITDA of roughly 2.6x. A re-rating toward even the lower band of peer multiples (around 5-6x EV/EBITDA) would be justified if NTG shows it can regrow its backlog and normalize margins. This suggests material upside from NTG's current price.

Risks

NTG is mostly concentrated in Saudi Arabia, with 95% of revenue coming from Saudi. That said, NTG is diversifying their geographical footprint with new commercial presence in Iraq, Oman, Egypt, and soon the UAE. Revenue outside Saudi Arabia is still small, but it is growing, helping the diversification case.

Another risk is the liquidity profile of the business. A lot of working capital is tied up in receivables and contract assets, with only about CAD$6M of cash on hand, but management communicated that they collected a lot of receivables, with positive operating cash flow expected in Q4/25. The 12% dilution last summer showed that growth is not fully self-funded. That said, management will be careful when it comes to capital deployment, as insider ownership stands at roughly 36%, which highlights alignment with shareholders.

Also, frameworks are economically meaningful but do not guarantee revenue, as NTG needs to sign firm purchase orders to validate revenue generation. We will monitor closely going into FY26 if they can continue announcing significant purchase orders to confirm momentum for the year.

We also keep an attentive eye on the backlog, which has remained elevated yet slightly declined YoY. This could potentially question NTG's growth potential moving forward, especially if that backlog were to shrink further.

Last but not least, NTG has recently communicated that the conflict in Iran did not have material disruption to operations or to their customer engagements. Their Egypt offshore centers are operating normally. This event can bring volatility, but the rollout of Vision 2030 is still moving ahead.

Conclusion

The market is pricing NTG like a fragile project-based contractor, while business is increasingly turning into a relationship-driven platform with strong account expansion, a differentiated Egyptian delivery engine, and significant traction within the Saudi ecosystem.

The key question is whether NTG can turn larger frameworks and expanded capacity into a larger backlog, feeding stronger revenue growth, normalized margins, and better operating cash flow in FY26. Given NTG's recent trajectory, we think it can. If so, then the current multiple appears too low.

data.title: Fidelity Select Energy Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The energy sector advanced a modest 0.90% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the MSCI U.S. IMI Energy 25/50 Index. More broadly, stocks in the U.S. gained 2.66% the past three months, according to the S&P 500® index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large-caps had only a slight advantage over small-caps.

Amid this backdrop, crude-oil prices – a key driver of profitability in the sector – trended downward in Q4. From start to finish, crude oil declined from about $62 per barrel to roughly $57 bbl, according to the West Texas Intermediate spot price, a benchmark for U.S. oil prices. Still, it remained at a level that continued to support profitability for many companies in the sector. Meanwhile, Henry Hub natural gas prices rose in Q4 to about $4 per Metric Million British Thermal Unit. The price of this commodity remained historically low, however, which continued to be somewhat of a headwind to profit growth for natural gas-related firms.

Among the industries within the MSCI sector index, oil & gas drilling companies, which represented about 1% of the sector index, fared the best, gaining about 17% this quarter. In this group, Transocean (RIG) (+32%), Helmerich & Payne (HP) (+31%) and Odfjell Drilling (+15%) stood out. Elsewhere, oil & gas equipment & services, which represented about 10% of the index, gained 8% in Q4, fueled by National Energy Services Reunited (NESR) (+53%), Halliburton (HAL) (+16%) and TechnipFMC (FTI) (+13%). Integrated oil & gas companies, the largest group at roughly 40% of the index, returned about 3%. Here, Exxon Mobil (XOM) (+8%) and Chevron (CVX) (-1%) had mixed results. In contrast, the oil & gas refining and marketing (-8%) and the oil & gas storage & transportation (-3%) lagged the broader index. ■

Performance Review

For the fourth quarter of 2025, the fund gained 1.33%, outpacing the MSCI U.S. IMI Energy 25/50 Index but trailing the S&P 500 index. Looking a bit longer term, performance comparisons over the trailing 1-, 3- and 5-year periods favor the fund over the sector benchmark.

Looking deeper into the fund's performance relative to the MSCI sector index, stock selection and an overweighting in the oil & gas equipment & services industry contributed to the fund's outperformance of the MSCI sector index in Q4. Within this segment, an out-of-benchmark stake in National Energy Services Reunited (+53%) was the fund's biggest individual relative contributor. In November, the Houston-based energy services firm reported solid earnings growth for the third quarter, which provided support for the stock price. In addition, an outsized position in TechnipFMC (+12%) also added relative value for the fund. TechnipFMC is an oilfield services company specializing in the manufacturing and installation of subsea equipment used in deepwater oil and gas production. Although oil prices declined in Q4, deepwater projects have been less affected because they are multi-year investments, and their profitability often breaks even at a very low oil price.

Conversely, an out-of-index stake in the independent power producers & energy traders industry notably weighed on the portfolio's relative result. More specifically, a sizable position in Vistra (VST), a power producer with operations in Texas and the mid-Atlantic, detracted, as the stock returned about -17% in Q4. We had conviction in Vistra's potential to benefit from rising demand for electric power. However, the company reported mixed quarterly financials results in November and narrowed its financial guidance for 2025. We increased our holdings a bit, and Vistra was the fund's No. 16 position at year-end. ■

Outlook and Positioning

Looking ahead, we expect oil prices to remain range-bound around $60 per barrel in the near term, barring any changes in the macroeconomic environment or significant geopolitical disruption. We anticipate a steady increase in global demand and slowing U.S. supply growth, as the OPEC+ consortium continues the process of bringing on spare capacity and recapturing market share. While geopolitical risk remains elevated, the global oil market is highly liquid and has been able to absorb short-term supply disruption. In the medium term, once all the OPEC+ spare capacity has been brought back online, we expect oil prices to strengthen, given that there is relatively little spare capacity in the market outside the Middle East, and the pipeline of new resources is fairly limited.

Meanwhile, we believe U.S. natural gas prices will likely be range-bound around $4 per MMBtu. While growth in demand from gas-fired power generation and the construction of export terminals is healthy, expanding supply also is significant, and the U.S. has sizable gas-production resources that will start to be deployed if the price of gas were to rise higher than $4 per MMBtu, likely putting a ceiling on how high prices can rise.

We believe rising demand for electricity – due to the build-out of new data centers – will remain an important trend in the power industry, with some spillover to natural gas markets over time.

In refining, we expect demand for gasoline and jet fuel to be resilient, particularly with the slowing adoption of electric vehicles, while refining capacity drifts downward due to refinery closures and new capacity coming online slower than anticipated. U.S. refining margins have the potential to remain structurally higher than has historically been the case, due to low prices for domestic natural gas and crude oil relative to non-U.S. refiners.

As of December 31, the fund's most prominent industry overweight is among oil & gas equipment & services stocks because we believe the outlook for capital spending in this segment is poised to improve. We're optimistic about prospects for several offshore oil services providers. In 2025, several large global supermajor oil producers sought to refresh their deepwater project pipelines. Offshore production has increased, particularly since the cost of development has declined sharply over the past decade, due to technology improvement. Further, expectations about when the world will reach peak oil demand have moved further into the future, giving corporate leaders license to approve investments in new, long-cycle deepwater projects.

With weaker oil prices year to date, onshore shale drilling activity has declined, whereas deepwater projects have remained more robust. Once a large deepwater development is underway, it's hard to interrupt it, and most of those projects have a relatively low breakeven oil price. As a result, their economics remain favorable in nearly all reasonable scenarios for the price of oil. The fund's biggest individual overweight positions in this group, as of year-end, are Cenovus Energy (CVE) and Canadian Natural Resources. ■

Fidelity® Select Energy Portfolio

Investment Approach

• Fidelity® Select Energy Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.

• We believe stocks can become mispriced relative to intrinsic value for a variety of reasons, including cyclically depressed earnings or overly positive or negative sentiment.

• Supported by in-depth fundamental research, we seek to uncover investment opportunities by analyzing the drivers of supply and demand for energy commodities, in combination with valuations and cash-generation potential for energy stocks.

• Our process is grounded in the belief that the ability to generate free cash flow over time is the best barometer of value, and that companies with quality businesses trading at attractive levels compared with future free-cash-flow generation tend to outperform.

• Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Killam Apartment REIT: East Coast Canadian Apartments On Sale
data.description:
Introduction

Killam Apartment REIT (KMP.UN:CA) is a Canadian multi-residential REIT focused on Eastern Canada. It sells for a sizeable discount to Net Asset Value (NAV), has a 4.5% dividend yield, and is expected to continue to grow Adjusted Funds From Operation (AFFO), providing an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

Properties

The majority of Killam's properties are located in Eastern Canada, with 60% of Net Operating Income (NOI) coming from the Atlantic provinces of Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador, and PEI. They also have a significant presence in Ontario and some properties in Alberta and British Columbia. Killam's properties are mainly apartments (89% of NOI), with 6% of NOI coming from Manufactured Home Communities (MHC) and 5% from what they call commercial properties, which are a mix of retail and office.

This makes Killam unique in the Canadian multi-residential REIT sector, as the majority of their peers either focus on the Greater Toronto Area (GTA), Montreal, and Ottawa, or Western Canada.

Main Markets
GTA, Montreal, Ottawa Western Canada Atlantic Canada
Killam X
InterRent X
Minto X
CAPREIT X
Boardwalk X
Mainstreet X

In recent years, Killam has felt the need to diversify their exposure away from Atlantic Canada. I was never a big fan of this, as it diluted my ability to get direct exposure to properties in this area of Canada, and there were enough other investment options in the areas they were expanding to. Recent rent growth has also been higher in Atlantic Canada than in Killam's other markets.

Thankfully, when Killam released their 2026 targets, they retired their diversification target, showing they have stopped prioritizing expanding in the rest of Canada...at least for now.

Net Asset Value

As I'm writing this, Killam trades at $16 a share, a significant discount to IFRS NAV and analyst-estimated NAV. Typically, the analyst-estimated NAV is more conservative for Canadian REITs, and in Killam's case, it's no different.

IFRS NAV reported in the 2025 annual report is ~$25 a share, meaning shares trade at 0.64x IFRS NAV. Analysts estimate a $22.5 NAV, equating to shares trading at 0.71x NAV.

Share Price NAV P/NAV
IFRS NAV (Annual Report) $16.00 $25.00 64%
Mean Analyst NAV (TIKR) $16.00 $22.50 71%

Historically, Killam has traded at an average of 0.92x analyst-estimated NAV, with long periods of time at or above NAV. Shares would need to move up 40% in order for them to trade at NAV again.

When looking at companies trading at significant discounts to NAV, I like to make sure the company is able to consistently increase NAV. If the company is not able to increase NAV, they aren't adding value to the business over time, and the market is less likely to value them at NAV, and you're more likely to be stuck in a value trap.

In the case of Killam, you can see over the long term they've been able to increase NAV (green line in graph below). So we've got a company that has a history of increasing NAV trading at a sizeable discount to NAV. Let's do a couple of checks to see if the analyst-estimated NAV is reasonable.

Is NAV Reasonable

In my opinion, the main reason to purchase Killam today is that you're able to purchase a quality apartment REIT trading at a significant discount to NAV and wait until it trades back up at NAV. To double-check that the analyst-estimated NAV is reasonable, let's compare it to a few other sources.

I calculate that the analyst NAV implies a cap rate of ~5.3% for Killam. As I highlighted in my previous article on CAPREIT, two Canadian multi-residential REITs have been taken private recently. InterRent (IIP.UN:CA) announced they were being taken private in May of 2025, and Minto (MI.UN:CA) announced in January 2026. These REITs were taken private at implied cap rates in the high 4s, significantly lower than the 5.3% used for Killam's NAV. Now, to be fair, we would expect the cap rate for Minto and InterRent's properties to be lower than Killam's due to the concentration of their properties in major markets versus the higher proportion of Killam's properties in secondary markets, but it does show there is a large appetite for Canadian apartments near a 5% cap rate. InterRent and Minto were taken private at prices equivalent to a 5% to 10% discount to the NAV these same analysts were estimating, giving more confidence in the NAV estimate for Killam.

Purchase Price / Analyst NAV (TIKR) Implied Purchase Cap Rate (Analyst Calculated)
Minto 89% 4.8%
InterRent 94% 4.7%

Using the most recent CBRE cap rates and the NOI breakdown by city from Killam's annual report, I arrive at a weighted average cap rate for Killam's portfolio of 4.8%-6.0%. The 5.3% analysts are using looks reasonable compared to the CBRE cap rates. It's slightly below the midpoint of the estimate, but Killam has been high-grading their portfolio by selling non-core older properties and developing newer ones, so I expect their properties to be above average quality.

Overall, the NAV estimate looks reasonable, and Killam and its insiders look like they agree. Just yesterday they announced that they are increasing their disposition target and repurchasing shares with a portion of the proceeds to take advantage of their NAV discount.

"The higher disposition target reflects favourable transaction pricing and a clear, disciplined capital allocation strategy. While Killam's Trust units trade at a discount to its per unit net asset value, the NCIB remains a compelling opportunity, complemented by selective acquisitions that enhance portfolio quality and long‑term value," noted Philip Fraser, President and CEO.

Manfred Walt, a Killam trustee, has been purchasing shares over the last few weeks, giving his vote of confidence as well.

Return Potential

The majority of the return potential for Killam is related to shares trading back up to NAV, but let's take a step back and look at what the return potential would be over the longer term if it stayed trading at the current discount.

The first source of return would be the dividend. The current yield is 4.5%, which is well covered with a 69% AFFO payout ratio.

A second source of return would be earnings growth from current properties. In 2025, same-property NOI growth was 6.1%, but this has been pushed higher than Killam's longer-term average due to the large increases in rent that occurred post-COVID. Due to the majority of Killam's units having rent control, it takes time for Killam to increase in-place rents up to market rates. Killam still has a 9% mark-to-market opportunity, but it's decreasing quickly as rents have been stagnating.

Longer term, I would expect same-property NOI/AFFO growth to track the rent growth they've achieved in the past. I'll assume they can achieve somewhere in the 2%-4% range. Killam's 2026 target of 3% same-property NOI growth for apartments and 2% consolidated NOI growth falls within this range even with the headwind from Sun Life vacating their Westmount Place commercial property.

With an AFFO payout ratio of 69%, you would expect there to be retained earnings that could also be reinvested for external growth. Taking a closer look at Killam's financial statements, though, all of the AFFO remaining after accounting for maintenance capex is spent on value-enhancing capital investments for their existing properties. This is mainly spent on building upgrades and suite renovations, which help increase rents for existing suites, but that benefit is already accounted for in our estimate for same-property earnings growth, so no incremental benefit here.

A third source of return would be earnings growth from developments (or acquisitions) done on a leverage-neutral basis. Even though Killam doesn't have retained earnings after dividend payments and capex on existing properties, earnings growth allows for taking on additional debt on a leverage-neutral (Debt/Gross Book Value) basis. Assuming current interest rates and development yields, I calculate that this could provide an additional 1% - 2% of earnings growth.

Killam does face debt financing headwinds, which will offset a portion of their earnings growth. I calculate that over the next 5 years this will average a headwind of ~1%. The majority of the impact occurs in 2026 and 2031.

Putting it all together, I calculate a long-term total annual return potential of 6.5% to 9.5%, assuming shares continue trading at the current discount to NAV. Overall, this return potential is reasonable for a defensive asset class like apartments but isn't something to get overly excited about.

Low High
Dividend Yield 4.5% 4.5%
Same Property AFFO Growth 2.0% 4.0%
Leverage Neutral Developments AFFO Growth 1.0% 2.0%
Debt Refinancing Impact on AFFO -1.0% -1.0%
Total Annual Return 6.5% 9.5%

To really get excited about an investment in Killam, you need to believe that cap rates will stay close to where they are now and that Killam will trade back at NAV. If Killam trades back at NAV in 3 years' time, it would add ~12% to your annualized return, while if it took 5 years, it would add 7%. This equates to an attractive total annualized return potential of ~15%-20%, depending on if it took 3 or 5 years. This assumes that cap rates stay as they are so NAV increases as earnings increase.

Risks

This opportunity exists because there are some real risks that are facing multi-residential REITs in Canada. Asking rents have been flat or dropping and are similar to where they were 3 years ago.

On the demand side for apartments, Canada's federal government is forecasting minimal population growth this year before returning to growth in 2027 and beyond.

There has also been an increase in supply in Killam's key markets, with Halifax and New Brunswick expected to deliver more multi-family units in 2026 and 2027 than the last 5-year average.

Bond yields have increased in the last few weeks as the war in Iran stokes inflation fears. If yields continue to rise, it could cause cap rates to expand and decrease Killam's NAV.

Conclusion

The supply and demand outlook for Killam over the next couple of years looks bleak, but if you take a longer-term view and believe that units will trade back at NAV sometime in the next 3-5 years, it leads to an attractive return profile. The current 4.5% yield makes it a little easier to be patient as the thesis plays out. I've recently added to Killam and may add more if it continues to drop.
